title: Private Equity and Financial Stability: Evidence from Failed-Bank Resolution in the Crisis
abstract: This paper investigates the role of private equity (PE) in failed-bank resolutions after the 2008 financial crisis, using proprietary Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation failed-bank acquisition data. PE investors made substantial investments in underperforming and riskier failed banks, particularly in geographies where local banks were also distressed, filling the gap created by a weak, undercapitalized banking sector. Using a quasi-random empirical design based on detailed bidding information, we show that PE-acquired banks performed better ex post, with positive real effects for the local economy. Overall, PE investors played a positive role in stabilizing the financial system through their involvement in failed-bank resolution.

article.introduction:
Private equity (PE) has becomean important component of the financial system. An extensive literature explores the effects of PE buyouts on firm-level outcomes, such as employment, productivity, product quality, and innovation. These papers often argue that such buyouts positively affect the operations of target companies.1Notwithstanding, the PE industry generates much controversy. Critics argue that PE transactions involve heavy financial engineering schemes that place a substantial debt burden on the target companies and hence increase default risk in the banking sector (Andrade and Kaplan (1998), Kaplan and Strömberg (2009)), particularly during economic downturns due to the cyclicality of PE investment (Bernstein, Lerner, and Mezzanotti (2019)).
Despite its importance, there has been little research on how PE interacts with and affects the stability of the financial system, especially during periods of crisis. In this paper we investigate this question by examining PE investors’ engagement in the failed-bank resolution process in the aftermath of the 2008 crisis. This is a novel setting in which to study PE and financial stability. First, while bank failures and their resolutions are an important feature of financial crises and have a significant real effect on the economy (Bernanke (1983), Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017)), they have received little academic attention to date. Our setting allows us to examine PE investors’ role in one of the most crucial steps in stabilizing the financial system during a crisis. Second, compared to industrial firms in which PE investors often intervene, banks are subject to more stringent regulation, and thus the expertise and human capital required for PE involvement in this setting is unique. How PE would fare in this regulatory environment is unknown, and thus warrants separate investigation. Third, while nonbank involvement in failed banks can be controversial, its participation can be significant. PE investors were very active in failed-bank auctions between 2008 and 2015, bidding for close to 100 failed banks in aggregate. Understanding PE's involvement in this setting is therefore important from both a regulatory and an academic perspective.
The role of PE in the resolution of failed banks is a priori unclear. On the one hand, there are concerns that PE investors could have a negative influence on financial stability. For instance, PE investors may take advantage of a fragile banking sector by targeting high-quality assets under fire sales or by exploiting banks’ safety nets and backstops. PE acquisitions may also be value-destroying if PE investors do not have the experience necessary to operate a bank.
On the other hand, PE investors could have a unique advantage in stabilizing the financial system through the acquisition of failed banks. First, PE investors have greater risk appetite, which may allow them to target riskier and lower quality failed banks that are less appealing to traditional bank acquirers. Under this line of reasoning, PE investors complement bank acquirers through the “selection” of failed banks. Second, during financial downturns, PE investors may have relatively more stable funding (Bernstein, Lerner, and Mezzanotti (2019)), which allows them to bring in new capital in times of capital scarcity. Third, PE investors may have the expertise to turn around distressed firms during adverse economic situations (Hotchkiss, Smith, and Strömberg (2021), Jiang, Li, and Wang (2012), Cohn, Hotchkiss, and Towery (2022)).
This paper exploits detailed proprietary failed-bank bidding and acquisition data from the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC). We observe information on the complete sample of failed banks in the United States between 2009 and 2014, including those acquired by PEs and those acquired by other existing banks. We identify PE investors who participated in failed-bank resolutions, both as bidders of the failed bank's assets in failed-bank auctions and as acquirers of the failed bank's assets when awarded the winning bid. PE acquisitions are significant, totaling about a quarter of all failed bank assets acquired in the 2009 to 2014 period. We augment these data with bank operation data extracted from the FDIC Consolidated Reports of Condition and Income “Call Reports,” the FDIC Summary of Deposits (SOD), and the FDIC Reports of Structure Change. We also supplement our data using PE-level information from Preqin.
Which failed banks do PE investors acquire? We find that on average PE investors bid for, and eventually acquire, failed banks that are larger and of poorer quality and higher risk compared to those acquired by other banks. PE-acquired banks are larger and more undercapitalized, as indicated by their lower tier 1 risk-based capital ratio (a measure of a bank's core equity capital), and have a lower core deposits ratio (a measure of the stability of a bank's source of funds). PE-acquired failed banks also have lower profitability prior to failure, as indicated by their net interest margin, and hold larger a proportion of riskier loans. For example, during the crisis, construction and development (C&D) loans had higher levels of underperformance, leading to an increase in credit risk ratings, and PE-acquired banks hold on average 27% more C&D loans than the 17.9% C&D loans to total loans in bank-acquired institutions.
PE investors also focus on banks that are less likely to immediately synergize with healthier existing banks. Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017) show that bank acquirers are more interested in purchasing failed banks that are geographically close to themselves to realize informational benefits and economies of scale. Hence, the natural acquirers of failed banks are healthy local banks. We show that failed banks whose neighboring banks are in worse health—when they have lower tier 1 capital, a higher proportion of C&D loans, and a higher proportion of other real estate owned (OREO), which can reflect larger amounts of distressed real estate properties held by the bank due to foreclosure—are more likely to be acquired by PE investors. We include additional analysis on the bidding activities of PE investors, which shows a similar investment approach of following a focused strategy of bidding on bank failures that many traditional banks are unlikely to be interested in or capable of acquiring.
Taken together, the evidence points to a sorting pattern of failed banks and their plausible acquirers: PE acquirers complement banks in the failed-banks market by bidding and ultimately acquiring lower quality and higher risk failed banks. In doing so, PE investors help channel capital that can fill the gap created by an undercapitalized banking sector and help meet the huge needs for new capital. PE presence can therefore allow more failed banks to avoid being liquidated and help preserve the local financial system.
We next ask how PE-acquired banks perform post-acquisition. As we document above, filling the gap in the failed-bank resolution process left by undercapitalized bank buyers is valuable. However, if PE interventions introduce excessive risk and long-term underperformance into the financial system, the overall impact of PE would have to be viewed with caution. The empirical challenge isolating the effect of PE on post-acquisition bank performance is the very acquisition selection pattern that we present above. Banks and PE investors target different segments of the market for failed banks, making any post-acquisition pattern a combination of treatment and selection.
To better identify the effect of PE acquirers, an ideal experiment would compare the post-acquisition performance of two otherwise similar banks, where one is quasi-randomly allocated to a PE investor and one to a bank. Our empirical strategy leverages proprietary FDIC bidding data for failed banks to generate a quasi-random sample. We are able to observe and leverage the identities of all bidders for each failed bank, the valuation of bids placed, and the relative positioning of those bids with respect to the FDIC reservation price to create samples of “close” bids. We proxy for the ideal experiment by focusing on a set of banks that were bid on by both PE investors and banks (i.e., selectable to both PE investors and banks) and whose bidding values were close (with a difference below 5% of total bank assets). Essentially, this exercise compares banks that were (marginally) won by bank acquirers and those (marginally) won by PE investors. This comparison allows us to significantly mitigate the confounding selection problem and more cleanly isolate the direct effect of PE intervention. Reassuringly, in this quasi-random sample, PE-acquired banks and bank-acquired banks look statistically identical along dozens of characteristics, and the winning probabilities of PE investors and banks in those auctions are nearly identical (52% vs. 48%). Characteristics of the auctions, such as number of bidders and the overall closeness of bidding offers, are also virtually identical.
This approach shares the same spirit as “close vote” analyses widely used in the political economy literature, in which researchers examine the effect of winning an election by comparing winners and losers of close elections. It is also similar to empirical design comparing winning and runner-up counties in attracting large manufacturing plants (Greenstone, Hornbeck, and Moretti (2010)), and comparing winning and losing bidders in an acquisition deal to study the long-run effect of mergers (Malmendier, Moretti, and Peters (2018)). Our design is potentially more desirable than designs subject to strategic political incentives and incumbency effects (De la Cuesta and Imai (2016)). Moreover, our strategy adds to existing identification strategies in the PE literature that make use of matching methods (e.g., Boucly, Sraer, and Thesmar (2011), Bernstein, Lerner, and Mezzanotti (2019), and Gupta et al. (2021)) or details in targets’ organization (Bernstein and Sheen (2016)).
Armed with this empirical strategy, we employ several different performance metrics. First, we track branch closures of acquired failed banks. PE-acquired failed-bank branches are less likely to close than bank-acquired failed-bank branches. This result is robust to our preferred specification in which we compare two bank branches that belong to two banks that failed in the same year and the same state. For these branches, the only difference is whether it was quasi-randomly won by another bank or a PE investor. It is important to note that the lower branch closing rate of PE-acquired banks is not due to the consolidation of local branches that often follow bank mergers, which would make the higher closing rate in bank acquisitions mechanical. In fact, we find a higher probability of exiting a county altogether and closure without a geographically close branch in bank-acquired banks, which cannot be explained merely by consolidation of local branches. We also find that PE-acquired banks experience a significantly higher increase (roughly 35% higher growth across specifications) in branch-level deposits compared to bank-acquired banks. Given deposits are the base for profits, lending capacity, and market power (Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022)), we interpret this result as a positive indicator for PE-acquired banks.
The ability to maintain the scale of operation and a robust deposit base is an important indicator of bank health and performance. More importantly, providing stable access to credit by operating a local branch has important consequences for local businesses and recovery from the financial crisis (Nguyen (2019)). The positive performance patterns of PE-acquired failed banks lead us to hypothesize that those acquisitions facilitate regional economic recovery from the crisis.
To examine the real effects of these failed-bank acquisitions, we adapt the quasi-random framework above to a county-level analysis. In this analysis, we compare two counties—each county has a failed-bank branch, but one is acquired by a PE investor and one is acquired by a bank. These counties are statistically identical prior to the acquisitions. During the post-acquisition period, however, those counties with PE acquisitions experience stronger recovery from the crisis, in particular, faster employment growth and increased total and per capita income. One potential channel for these effects is the sustained lending activity supported by the acquired bank branches. Compared to bank-intervened counties, PE-intervened counties observe higher growth in small business lending, in terms of both the number of loans and the amount, and the loans in those counties are issued at a lower interest rate. The active small business lending is part of PE-acquired banks’ shift to commercial and industrial (C&I) lending from distressed real estate-related lending.
The positive performance that we document may be due in part to the expertise of the management team that PE investors bring into failed banks. Using hand-collected data on CEOs appointed at banks after PE acquisitions, we show that PE investors hire ex-bankers who have, on average, nearly 30 years of experience in the banking industry, and more than half of them were CEOs of other banks before being appointed at the failed banks. Moreover, more than 60% of the CEOs had experience in the local area of the failed bank, more than a third specialized in turnaround management and troubled and distressed assets, and about a third had previously founded a bank that eventually merged with a larger buyer. We show that on average PE investors hold acquired failed banks for 6.5 years before exiting by selling to another bank or going public through initial public offering (IPO). The majority of the exits occur by selling to a local bank, that is, a bank that overlaps with the footprint of the acquired bank at the zip code level.
Taken together, our results suggest that PE plays an important role in the resolution of failed banks in times of crisis. PE investors provide much-needed capital in acquisitions, which helps fill the gap in a weak banking sector at a time when the natural potential acquirers—local banks—are themselves in distress. PE acquirers also help turn around acquired failed banks, preserving their branch structure, helping deposit growth, and managing the loan portfolio losses, with positive effects for the real economy. Although there are natural policy concerns in allowing PE firms to form bank holding companies and in bringing nonbanks into banking in general, our results suggest that private financial investors can play a positive role in resolving failed banks in a crisis.
This paper connects to several strands of literature. First, it is related to an extensive literature that studies the role of PE in the economy (e.g., Kaplan (1989), Lichtenberg and Siegel (1990), John, Lang, and Netter (1992), Boucly, Sraer, and Thesmar (2011), Lerner, Sorensen, and Strömberg (2011), Cohn, Hotchkiss, and Towery (2022), Bernstein et al. (2016), Bernstein and Sheen (2016), DeYoung, Kowalik, and Torna (2021), Sorensen and Yasuda (2023)), particularly in some regulated industries (e.g., Eaton, Howell, and Yannelis (2020), Kirti and Sarin (2024), Gandhi, Song, and Upadrashta (2023), Gupta et al. (2021)). These studies tend to focus on firms and almost exclusively on normal times, while our focus is on banks and distressed states of the world. The positive effect of PE intervention that we identify is consistent with prior studies that find positive effects of PE investors on firm productivity (Davis et al. (2014)), on turning around distressed situations (Hotchkiss, Smith, and Strömberg (2021)), and on liquidity provision during the financial crisis (Bernstein, Lerner, and Mezzanotti (2019)). However, the behavior of PE investors in a highly regulated and sensitive industry such as banking is likely different from the behavior of PE investors in other sectors. Our key contribution is to address the role of PE on financial stability by directly examining the interaction between PE and banks, particularly failed banks, which can result in significant costs to the financial system.
This paper also relates to an emerging body of work examining the behavior of financial institutions during the financial crisis (e.g., Berger and Bouwman (2013)) and the resolution of failed banks and their impact (James and Wier (1987), James (1991), Vij (2019)).  Closest to our work is Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017), who provide the key economic insight that poor capitalization of local banks, which are the natural buyers of failed banks, results in substantial costs to the FDIC's resolution efforts for failed banks. Our paper contributes to this new area of research by introducing PE investors into the picture, which buy a surprising 24% of the failed-bank assets acquired in the resolution process during the sample period. We show that PE investors operate as a complementary source of funding in the resolution of bank failures at a time when local bank buyers are themselves undercapitalized. Further, we show that PE acquisitions perform well and benefit the real economy. From a policy perspective, our findings support the policy innovation of the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) and the FDIC that allowed financial buyers to participate in the resolution of failed banks. In many emerging economies, governments play a larger role in both bank ownership and in managing bank distress. Our results suggest that PE investors bring additional capital that can lower the need/cost of government intervention.2To the extent that PE investors play a positive role in acquiring and operating failed banks, our findings suggest this can be a potentially viable option to consider in financial crises or downturns.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIprovides institutional background on failed-bank resolution. SectionIIdescribes the data used for analysis. SectionIIIpresents evidence on PE's role in failed-bank resolution. SectionIVexamines the post-acquisition performance of PE-acquired and bank-acquired failed banks, and the real effects for local economic recovery. Finally, SectionVconcludes.
article.1 Institutional Background:
In this section, we provide background on how the FDIC resolves bank failures and how PE investors can become a qualified participant in this process. Roughly 500 banks failed during the financial crisis, putting a large strain on the financial system. This can be seen clearly in Figure1, which plots the number of bank failures (Panel A) and total assets at failure (Panel B) from the year 2000 onward.
When a bank fails, the FDIC is appointed receiver by the failing institution's chartering authority. To address bank failures, the FDIC used the Purchase and Assumption (P&A) resolution method in roughly 95% of cases from 2009 to 2014; these transactions form the basis of our study in this paper.3In a P&A transaction, the FDIC uses a process that resembles a first-price sealed bid auction to sell some or all of the assets and liabilities of the depository institution. Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017) provide a detailed description of how the process of failed-bank resolution works. We review relevant points here and refer interested readers to their paper and to the FDIC'sResolutions Handbook4for further details. Figure2illustrates this process. We also provide more details in AppendixB.
To sell a failed bank, the FDIC first generates a broad list of potential bidders that have expressed interest in bidding for failed institutions and that meet a list of requirements, such as satisfactory capital and supervisory ratings. Upon signing a confidentiality agreement, interested parties may be granted access to a virtual data room for the review of available information, which can include loan information, schedules representing the book value of items on the failed bank's balance sheet, and operational information. Interested parties can place sealed bids for the failed bank based on standard transaction terms, resembling a first-price sealed bid auction. Using its proprietary least-cost test,5the FDIC selects the bid whose terms entail the lowest estimated cost for the Deposit Insurance Fund (DIF) if those costs are below the cost of liquidation estimated by the FDIC, which is unknown to bidders. The bank is then closed, and the FDIC transfers assets and deposits to the acquirer.6
In the past, private investors have generally been excluded from bidding on failed institutions because they themselves were not chartered banks. However, in 2008 the OCC began making it easier for PE investors to participate in failed-bank auctions by creating a “shelf charter” program. A shelf charter allows investors to bid on failed banks despite not being affiliated with an existing bank. The OCC also made available an “inflatable charter” that allows PE investors to buy a small, nonfailing bank with the intention of growing it quickly through subsequent failed-bank acquisitions. There were 20 distinct charters, obtained by 19 PE consortia (we discuss consortia more below), used to acquire failed banks by PE in the crisis.
In the charter approval process, PE acquisition plans are subject to a high degree of regulatory scrutiny. Potential PE investors must submit details such as the proposed bank management team qualifications, the sources and amount of capital available to the bank, and the business plan describing intended operations of the acquired bank. The charter information-gathering, application, and approval process typically occurs well in advance of any specific acquisition opportunities that may arise. In addition, because PE firms engage in nonbanking activities, they must also apply to the Federal Reserve Board for preliminary approval to form a bank holding company (BHC).
Most PE investors apply for a bank charter and form a BHC as a consortium together with a number of other PE firms, often in the form of an LLC with PE firms as shareholders. This can allow greater access to capital and help leverage relevant expertise and connections. From a BHC regulatory perspective there are advantages as well—firms with less than a 25% share of voting securities or up to one-third of total equity (provided this includes less than 15% of voting securities) are not subject to BHC regulation by the Federal Reserve Board. Applying as a consortium can help limit the equity investments of individual PE firms below these regulatory thresholds. For example, a number of PE firms might form an LLC with equity shares of the individual PE firm participants below the threshold. The LLC is then used for the proposed BHC and serves as the actual acquiring entity. Thus, while the holding company formed by the PE firms is exposed to BHC regulation, the individual PE firms themselves typically are not.
PE acquirers did not have restrictions on what they could bid on, aside from the usual criteria applied to other bank bidders, such as available capital and financial health. However, PE acquirers did have additional requirements imposed by the FDIC for operating the bank. Notably, PE acquirers were required to maintain a tier 1 common equity to total assets ratio of at least 10% for at least three years after the time of acquisition. They also had to maintain continuity of ownership for at least three years after the time of acquisition, absent FDIC approval. Further, they were not permitted to engage in any credit transactions with affiliates, which explicitly rules out using the bank to lend to affiliated companies or investments in which the PE firm has invested equity. Additional PE requirements included: pledges of cross-support if investors own 80% or more of two or more banks; no ownership by entities in bank secrecy jurisdictions; no opaque ownership structures; no investors with 10% or more equity in the bank before failure; and required disclosure about investors and ownership to the FDIC.7
As Figure3shows, PE investors acquired about 8.5% of the banks that failed in 2009 (11 out of 129). That number is larger in terms of assets, with PE investors acquiring 25.2% of the total failed-bank assets that were acquired in the resolution process that year.8PE investors remained active in acquiring failed banks through 2014, and through all of those years, they acquired 13% of all failed banks acquired, or 24% in terms of assets acquired in resolution.9After 2014, PE investors stopped acquiring failed banks. This might be due to two reasons. First, bank failures returned to pre-crisis levels (below 10 every year in number), leaving limited room for PE investors to pursue these investments. Second, PE funds formed during the crisis to pursue the strategy of failed-bank acquisitions reached the latter part of their typical 10- to 14-year life cycle, and thus started to focus more on operations and exit. Our main analytical sample covers the 2009 to 2014 period.10TableIA.Iin theInternet Appendixprovides a breakdown of these deals by year.11
article.2 Data and Sample Overview:
Between 2009 and 2014, 482 bank failures were resolved by the FDIC. PE acquirers purchased 62 of these failed banks, while existing banks purchased 393 of the failed banks.12Twenty-five failed banks with no least-cost bid were liquidated by the FDIC, and another two provided correspondent banking services to client banks only and were temporarily run by the FDIC as bridge banks after failure; we exclude these 27 banks from our analysis following Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017). Details on bank failures and their resolutions come from the FDIC. The data include public information on the timing of failure and acquisition, the location and size of the institution at failure, the amount of assets passed to the acquirer, and the terms of the failed-bank transaction (transaction type, loss share coverage, etc.). We also obtain proprietary FDIC information on the identities of the acquirer and of all the bidders and details on the bids.
We use proprietary FDIC cost estimates in evaluating failed-bank bids. This includes the FDIC's estimated liquidation cost for the failed bank and the valuation of the winning and cover bids from the internal least-cost analysis. We use these estimates to identify close bids for the same failed bank, which are useful for creating a quasi-random empirical design to isolate the effect of PE intervention. These data are also useful to run back-of-the-envelope calculations of the difference in cost savings had PE investors been excluded from the auction process.
Finally, we obtain confidential FDIC data on loan performance in loss share portfolios. Loss share participants are required to submit regular reports to the FDIC on the performance of loans covered by shared loss agreements and details on any losses incurred. Because acquired banks are typically absorbed into healthy existing banks, it is difficult to track the performance of failed-bank assets once they have been acquired. However, the loss share performance data allow us to observe the performance of those assets over time, even after acquisition. This provides us with a unique and important window into the management of failed-bank assets by PE and bank acquirers.
We obtain data on the financial characteristics of failed banks and their acquirers from the FDIC's quarterly Call Reports and from Thrift Financial Reports for institutions regulated by the Office of Thrift Supervision prior to 2012. These reports contain details on income and balance sheet characteristics for all of the banks in our sample.
The SOD database provided by the FDIC allows us to obtain bank branch location data for each commercial and savings bank and the level of deposits. In particular, the SOD database contains information on the geographical coordinates, address, and deposits of each branch of every commercial and savings bank operating in the United States. We complement these data using the FDIC's Reports of Structure Changes, which provide information on branch office openings and closings. Taken together, these sources allow us to analyze the closing of failed-bank branches, and to track changes in deposits at the county level within the footprint of the failed bank. Following Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), we use RateWatch to obtain branch-level data on deposit rates.
The geographic information on bank branches also allows us to capture characteristics of the neighboring banks of each failed institution. Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017) show that neighboring banks’ financial health is an important determinant of the failed-bank resolution process. Consistent with their paper, we define local banks as banks whose branch network overlaps at least one zip code with the branch network of the focal failed bank.
Our classification of PE acquisitions comes from the charter used to bid on failed banks, namely, acquisitions made by nonbank bidders that obtained a shelf or inflatable charter for the purpose of bidding on failed banks. Although most of the funds raised by these nonbank acquirers are from PE firms, they sometimes include non-PE sources such as asset management companies, institutional investors, and family offices. However, PE firms are the key participant in this process. For example, based on information in Securities and Exchange Commission registration statements and FDIC depository insurance applications, we estimate that PE sources account for more than 70% of the ownership of each consortium on average, when focusing on consortia participants identified as having 5% holdings or more. In addition, the vast majority of acquisitions conducted by those private investor consortia were led by PE firms.13InInternet AppendixTableIA.IV, we employ alternative definitions of PE-acquired banks based on the level of PE ownership (e.g., PE shares greater than or equal to 75%) and find that our results continue to hold.
We collect additional information about individual PE funds from several sources. First, we manually match the PE funds with information from Preqin. Preqin collects quarterly aggregated investments, distributions, and net asset values (NAVs) of PE funds as recorded by U.S. pension funds and obtained via routine Freedom of Information Act requests. For funds covered by the Preqin data, we collect information on the size of the fund, the vintage year, and the background of the PE firm. We supplement these data by searching for additional information from PitchBook and Crunchbase, and by conducting a general Internet search.
For information on local employment and startup creation, we use data from the U.S. Census Quarterly Workforce Indicators (QWI) to compute total employment by firm age and by county. The QWI is derived from the Longitudinal Employer-Household Dynamics (LEHD) program at the Census Bureau. It provides total employment in the private sector in each county as well as employment broken down by different firm age categories. Income data at the county level come from the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) Statistics of Income and is measured in calendar years (i.e., January to December of each year).
For local access to small business credit, we use small business lending data obtained from the Small Business Administration (SBA). We use contract-level information on 7(a) loans, one of the largest SBA programs, delivered through various methods (Certified Lenders Program, Preferred Lenders Program, SBA Express, etc.). We focus on both the quantity and the pricing of SBA loans made in each region-year.
TableIprovides summary statistics from the PE side. In Panel A, we provide statistics at the consortium level, which is the relevant unit for failed-bank purchases. As described above, multiple PE funds typically join forces to form a consortium and obtain a bank charter. On average, each bank acquisition consortium consists of around 3.4 PE investors, focusing only on those with shares of 5% or more. On average, each consortium bids on 5.2 failed banks14and “wins” 3.26 of them. The bidding strategies of the consortia are quite focused, and most consortia purchase banks within one state. Interestingly, 58% of PE consortia include at least one PE firm that has actively participated in the distressed investment space as identified in Hotchkiss, Smith, and Strömberg (2021) and Jiang, Li, Wang (2012), suggesting that these PE firms have some experience in distressed investments and turnarounds.
In Panel B, we provide summary statistics at the individual PE level, conditioning on data availability from Preqin. On average, each PE firm joins 1.13 consortia. Thus, only around 10 PE firms joined more than one consortium. In some cases, multiple funds in the same PE firm joined the same consortium. Those PE investors are traditional independent PE investors and are not affiliated with other banks. Importantly, most of the investing funds are of recent vintages. The median fund vintage is 2007, and more than 25% of the PE funds are formed in or after 2009. These results suggest that those funds had relatively adequate funding for such actions. Most of these funds are not first-time funds, so they have accumulated networks and human capital from prior activity in the PE markets. When a large PE fund joins an acquiring consortium, this line of business may not be the only business that the fund engages in. In Panel B,Fund sizeindicates the size of total funds raised for each fund that participated in at least one bank-acquiring consortium. The median fund in our sample raised $1.4 billion.15
TableIIreports summary statistics for failed banks as of the last quarter prior to failure. Most failed banks in the crisis were small community banks. The median bank size was just above $200 million, but the size distribution has a very long right tail.16Unsurprisingly for banks that failed, the tier 1 risk-based capital ratios just prior to failure were very low—far below the average for other neighboring banks that did not fail. Liquid assets were just under 17% of total assets on average, and core deposits comprised almost 84% of total deposits. These banks tended to be heavily involved in commercial real estate (CRE) lending, making up almost 39% of the total loans on their balance sheets. They also held a large amount of C&D loans, comprising almost 19% of their total loans on average. The ratio of C&I loans to total loans was smaller at 10%, and the ratio of consumer loans to total loans was just over 2%. Residential lending averaged 25.9% of total loans. In addition, both the noncurrent loans to total loans and the OREO assets to total assets of the failed banks were much higher than other neighboring banks, indicating portfolios in distress. Two-thirds of the banks were resolved using shared loss agreements with the FDIC.Internet AppendixTableIA.IIreports summary statistics by PE acquisitions and bank acquisitions.

article.3 Private Equity in Failed-Bank Resoluti:
We first investigate which segment of the failed-bank market was targeted by PE investors, and which was targeted by banks. The result of this analysis indicates whether PE investors complement incumbent banks as buyers of failed banks.
The analysis uses the cross-sectional sample of all failed banks that were eventually acquired by a bank or a PE investor. The dependent variable is a dummy that takes a value of 1 if the failed bank was eventually acquired by PE, and 0 otherwise (acquired by a bank). We control for the size of the bank using the logarithm of total assets at failure. Year-quarter fixed effects are included to account for time-varying economic, market, and regulatory factors that could affect the failed-bank resolution process.
TableIII, Panel A, presents the results, reporting marginal effects calculated at the sample mean. Column (1) concerns the tier 1 risk-based capital ratio. Tier 1 capital includes the most loss-absorbing forms of capital, and hence is an important representation of bank financial strength. The marginal effect of –0.013 suggests that, for a one-standard-deviation decrease in the tier 1 risk-based capital ratio, there is a 3.5-percentage-point increase in the probability that the bank is purchased by PE. This is a 25.8% increase from the base rate of 13.6%. Bank size significantly positively correlates with the probability that the bank is acquired by PE, indicating that PE investors may have had an advantage over existing banks in raising larger amounts of capital for these purchases.

In column (2) we employ another measure of the stability of banks’ sources of funding, namely, the ratio of core deposits to total deposits. Core deposits are made in a bank's natural demographic market and offer advantages to banks, such as predictable costs, low sensitivity to short-term changes in interest rates, and lower run risks. The core deposits to total deposits ratio is negatively associated with PE acquisition. For a one-standard-deviation decrease in core deposits to total deposits, the coefficient of –0.220 implies a 23.4% increase from the base rate of PE acquisition.
Column (3) shows that low-profitability failed banks are more likely to be acquired by PE investors. We use the net interest margin, which captures how much a bank earns in interest compared to how much it pays out as a ratio of interest-earning assets, as an indicator of bank profitability. This measure helps mitigate potential measurement noise for failing banks that other profitability measures like ROA or ROE may suffer. For example, interest-earning assets should be less wildly variable than total assets used in ROA calculations in the face of large, discrete charge-offs in nonperforming assets. Moreover, it is not uncommon for average equity to realize negative values in banks that are failing, in which case ROE is not reported. Net interest income should also be less variable than the total net income used for calculating ROA and ROE, due to the provisioning behavior for loan and lease losses (which are treated as an expense to net income on the bank's income statement) at failing banks.
Columns (4) and (5) examine the loan composition of banks. Banks with riskier asset portfolios, as captured by a higher proportion of C&D loans and OREO assets to total assets, were more likely to be acquired by PE investors. C&D loans can be more sensitive to economic or market conditions than other types of loans for several reasons. For example, projects may experience budget overruns or a lack of timely progress, market conditions can change drastically in the time between loan approval and project completion, and lenders may have greater difficulty extracting collateral value from an incomplete construction project in default. In the financial crisis, C&D loans turned out to be particularly problematic. As the FDIC describes in its history of the crisis (FDIC (2017)), “Most banks that failed or became problem banks did so because of large concentrations, relative to their capital, of poorly underwritten and administered commercial real estate loans and (especially) ADC [acquisition, development & construction] loans” (p. 179). Similarly, a higher proportion of OREO assets is often a sign of a loan portfolio in distress.
Not surprisingly, these measures of bank health (from both asset and liability sides) are correlated (seeInternet AppendixTableIA.III). However, the correlations are not particularly high for most of the pairs. In column (6), the analysis includes all bank characteristics in the regression analysis. We find that all measures except OREO to total assets remain statistically significant.
Overall, Panel A suggests that PE investors capture a set of banks that tend to be riskier in terms of asset composition, more undercapitalized, and less profitable, and they may require larger capital injections due to their size. Moreover, these banks have lower profitability as indicated by their net interest margin. These features are all likely undesirable in the eyes of incumbent banks. In this sense, PE investors target banks that other banks may be less willing or able to take over.
Next, we further strengthen the argument that PE investors acquire a segment of banks that are unlikely to be taken over and operated by other incumbent banks. In recent work on failed-bank resolution in the crisis, Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017) show that healthier banks are more willing to bid on and pay higher amounts for failed banks that they could potentially create synergies with. A primary source of synergistic benefit is in the form of geographic clustering—a large literature shows that the transmission of soft information depreciates with geographic distance (Petersen and Rajan (2002), Stein (2002)). Moreover, geographically closer banks may be better able to benefit from economies of scale to achieve operational efficiencies. The implication of this work is that failed banks without a set of healthier banks in nearby geographic regions face greater challenges in the resolution process. This is particularly true when, as TableIII, Panel A, shows certain failed banks had below-average performance and held riskier asset portfolios that other banks may not have desired.
Is PE participation particularly important for those banks without a potential bank acquirer? We explore this question in a similar setting as in equation (1), except the key explanatory variables are the conditions of the local banks. We create a set of measures to capture different dimensions of local bank conditions, where “local” are those banks whose branch network overlaps at least one zip code with the branch network of the focal failed bank.
TableIII, Panel B, presents the results. In column (1), we focus on neighboring banks’ tier 1 risk-based capital ratio, which is calculated as the mean of this ratio for all banks local to the failed banks. We find a negative and significant coefficient, which means that when a failed bank's neighboring banks, which are often considered the most likely acquirers, are in worse financial health, the failed bank is more likely to be acquired by PE. The economic magnitude is quite large: a one-standard-deviation decrease in the average neighboring bank tier 1 risk-based capital ratio is associated with a 2.92-percentage-point increase in PE acquisition probability, which is an increase of 21% from the base rate. This result holds using other bank health measures such as noncurrent loans (column (2)) and OREO (column (3)).
In column (4), we consider another factor that could affect the capability of acquisition by local banks—the size of these banks. Even if a neighboring bank remains healthy, it is unlikely to obtain enough capital to purchase anything that is comparable to or greater than its own size. In other words, banks that are considerably larger than the failed bank may be more likely to make the purchase. We show this using the variableNo. of Local Banks (>3xSize), which is the number of local banks that are at least three times the size of the failed bank in terms of total assets. The larger this number is, the more likely the bank will be sold to another bank and less likely to a PE acquirer.
In column (5), we examine the number of failed banks in the region. If a failed bank is in a region where a lot of other banks failed, they are more likely to be acquired by PE. This could be because other local banks are on average less healthy and less capable of making the purchase as well as because it is difficult for any acquirer to purchase a large number of failed banks if too many fail in the same region. We show that the total number of failed banks in a state is associated with a higher probability that the bank is acquired by PE. In other words, PE investors inject new capital in regions where on average banks are in deeper distress. InInternet AppendixTableIA.V, we show that both failed banks’ own characteristics and their neighboring banks’ characteristics matter when they enter the analysis simultaneously.
The results so far are based on final bidding outcomes. But is there evidence that PE investors are actively pursuing this strategy, or are PE investors just passively bidding on all failed banks and winning only the low-quality ones?
TableIVpresents evidence that is consistent with PE investors actively pursuing such a strategy. We obtain this evidence by examining not only the winning PE acquisitions, but also the bidding histories of each PE. To do so, we leverage proprietary data on all bidding histories in FDIC failed-bank auctions. We create the variablePE Bidthat indicates whether there were any PE bids on the specific failed bank, regardless of whether PE or a bank achieved the winning bid. The indicator captures PE bids that are above the liquidation value and nonpublic details on those that fall below, so it reflects all bidding activity by PE acquirers. We use thePE Bidvariable as the dependent variable following the same framework as in equation (1).

All of the variables that are associated with the final acquisition outcomes, as shown in TableIII, also have similar effects in explaining the bidding activities of PE investors. Clearly, both the decision to bid and the outcome of winning are equilibrium outcomes that account for the value and costs of the failed banks to different potential buyers and to the FDIC. Nevertheless, the evidence clearly suggests that PE investors actively, not passively, participate in the process.
In Figure4, we push the argument further by depicting the sorting and matching between acquirer types and bank characteristics. The graph compares characteristics of failed banks and their neighboring banks across banks that are targeted by different acquirers—those bid on and acquired by PE investors, those bid on by PE investors but eventually won by a PE entity or an incumbent bank, and those that are only bid on and eventually acquired by other incumbent banks. For each characteristic, we separately show the mean across the three groups.
Figure4shows a clear sorting pattern. Panel A shows sorting on failed-bank characteristics. PE investors bid on and successfully acquire the weakest banks (left bars) whether measured by tier 1 capital ratio, core deposits, net interest margin, or real estate owned. PE investors also target some slightly stronger failed banks over which they compete with other banks, and they may win or lose in such cases (middle bars). In contrast, banks typically focus on the healthier banks in the failed-bank set (right bars), and PE investors do not even bid in this segment. Panel B shows sorting on neighboring bank characteristics. Again, both PE bids and PE acquisitions are more likely when the neighboring banks are weak (whether measured by tier 1 capital ratios, nonperforming loans, real estate owned, or the number of failed banks in the state). This again confirms that PE investors complement bank acquirers in the market. This result also suggests that even though neighboring banks are likely the best potential buyers for failed banks operation-wise, they may be hesitant to bid and/or purchase when the bank is particularly risky or in deeper distress. PE investors have an edge in such cases due to their higher risk appetite and greater funding.
Overall, the evidence above provides additional support that PE investors complement existing players in the banking sector in acquiring failed banks. Specifically, they acquire underperforming banks that are riskier, particularly when the failed bank is in a region where neighboring banks experience deeper distress and thus are unlikely to be able bid. InInternet AppendixTableIA.VI, we provide further evidence on the bidding behavior of PE investors, showing that they follow an active strategy of bidding on those failed banks that many traditional banks are unlikely to be interested in or capable of acquiring.
article.4 Bank Performance Post PE Acquisition:
The next question that arises is: are PE investors able to successfully turn around the failed banks? In this section, we examine the post-acquisition performance of failed banks that are acquired by PE and by other banks. We also study the real effects by examining the economic recovery of regions in which the failed banks are acquired by PE investors. The performance of PE acquisitions compared to bank acquisitions is a priori unclear. On the one hand, PE investors do not have bank-specific operational experience or sources of synergies, which may lead to poorer performance. On the other hand, PE investors’ experience in distressed investment and turnaround operations (Hotchkiss, Smith, and Strömberg (2021)), together with the more stable financing source (Bernstein, Lerner, and Mezzanotti (2019)), may be well suited for the turnaround of failed banks in crisis.
To study failed banks’ performance changes post-acquisition by different buyers, a basic regression would simply compare the performance proxies in PE-acquired and bank-acquired banks following the acquisition event. This specification would be informative but masks the combination of (i) the sorting of acquirer-target, as documented in the previous section, and (ii) the treatment effect of PE acquisitions on acquired banks. An ideal experiment to isolate the effect of PE investors on post-acquisition performance would focus on the subset of failed banks that are of interest to both PE investors and banks and for which the allocation to a PE investor or a bank is nearly random.
We approximate an ideal experiment by exploiting a small set of banks for which acquirer allocation can be viewed as quasi-random in a setting of close bids. We start from the sample of banks that were bid on by both PE investors and banks. We narrow this sample down to two sets of banks—those won by PE that had at least one bank bid, and those won by a bank that had at least one PE bid. We further require that in the auction, the margin of victory of the winning PE/bank bidder over the closest losing bank/PE bid is small (within 5%).17Our empirical strategy compares failed banks that were just won by PE investors and those that were just won by banks in the quasi-random sample. This approach shares the same spirit as “close vote” analyses widely established in the political economy literature, where researchers examine the effect of winning an election by comparing winners and losers of close elections.
The key identification assumption is that, for the specific subset of competitive auctions that we consider, whether a PE bidder wins or loses the auction is “as good as” random. At first glance, of the 48 acquisition deals that fall into our quasi-random sample, 25 were eventually acquired by a PE investor and 23 by a bank, which suggests a fairly random pattern. TableVpresents a balance test. We first examine whether bank-level characteristics differ between the bank-acquired and PE-acquired samples. Among those characteristics shown to matter in broader selection, none seem to matter in this quasi-random sample. We also compare the variables that we use as performance proxies below—these include branch-, bank-, or regional-level information. Again, we do not find significant differences across bank-acquired and PE-acquired samples. Results on auction-level characteristics, such as the number of bidders and the overall closeness of bidding offers, are also reported toward the end of the table. These characteristics are comparable across the PE-acquired and the bank-acquired samples.
Our empirical strategy improves on common approaches in the PE literature that tackle the identification challenge through building counterfactual cases using matching methods.18Instead of assuming a matched firm that is equally selectable by PE, we leverage the auction setting in which PE bids and bank bids reveal their interests in selecting a commonly known target. Focusing on the sample of banks that are selectable and tightly bid on by both PE investors and banks further controls for selection effects. This design is potentially more desirable than the regression discontinuity design commonly used in political science and political economy studies that exploit close elections as they are often subject to strategic political incentives and incumbency effects (see De la Cuesta and Imai (2016) for a comprehensive review).
One caveat is that even though this approach exploits a tight control group, it is not a fully specified regression discontinuity design in which we would better control for micro-level variation in the bidding gaps (i.e., the running variable), which are unobserved by us. In our sample, the continuous bidding gap is coded into three categories by the FDIC—below 5%, 5% to 10%, and above 10%—while the underlying continuous variable is kept proprietary.
We first study bank performance post-acquisition. This analysis is conducted at the branch level for data observability reasons. In particular, failed banks acquired by other banks are integrated into the acquiring institution, and thus it is difficult to observe standalone performance measures from standard sources such as Call Reports. To overcome this problem, in this section we focus on the performance of bank branches that can be tracked prior to and after an acquisition.
Our first performance analysis concerns the closing of bank branches. Bank branch closure has important consequences for access to credit in the local economy and thus for financial stability. Nguyen (2019) shows that access to credit is very local, and bank branch closings lead to a persistent decline in local small business lending. This effect is particularly strong during the financial crisis.
Our sample includes all bank branches owned by a failed bank at the time of failure. For the key outcome variable, we code whether the bank branch is closed within three years post-acquisition using FDIC Reports of Structure Changes.19Control variables include those that have strong power in explaining PE investors’ acquisition selection—tier 1 risk-based capital ratio, core deposits to total deposits, C&D loans to total loans, and OREO to total assets. Fixed effects are included at the failure year × state level. Standard errors are clustered at the state and year of failure levels.20
TableVIreports the results. Columns (1) and (2) are our preferred specifications, which use the quasi-random sample. In column (1), we consider a thought experiment in which two bank branches are located in the same geographic region (at the level of state) and whose parent banks failed in the same year. This analysis allows us to remove the potential impact of timing, that is, banks employing a different operational strategy during different points in the crisis. We also remove the potential impact of branch location, since branches located in regions hit harder by the crisis may be more likely to close. The coefficient of –0.148 in column (1) means that bank branches acquired by PE investors are 14.8 percentage points less likely to be closed. This is 73.6% lower than the branch closing rate of 20.1% across the full sample.
This result is particularly important to understand the role of PE acquisitions in stabilizing the local financial system, considering our earlier finding that PE-acquired banks tend to be located in regions with an unhealthy financial sector, as shown in TableIV. To better illustrate this point, in column (2) we explore a new dependent variable that captures cases in which failed banks close and exit from a county. This variable takes the value of 1 if an acquired bank branch closes and no other branch of the failed bank or the acquiring bank serves the county. The coefficient of –0.070 suggests that PE-acquired banks are less likely to close and exit a county that they serve.
Column (2) also provides an opportunity to assess another possible explanation for PE-acquired banks’ lower probability of closing branches, namely, that bank-acquired failed banks are more likely to have branches in regions in which the acquirer already has a branch. In other words, bank acquirers may shut down an acquired branch because they already own one in the local area (i.e., consolidation of branches). The results in column (2) suggest that branches of bank-acquired failed banks have a higher frequency of closing, even in cases in which the closure means a complete exit from the county.
A remaining concern is that branch consolidation may occur in a manner that is not perfectly captured by county boundaries, so we ensure that exits from counties do not occur when the acquirer has a branch in a county that is nearby. InInternet AppendixTableIA.IX, we extend the analysis in TableVIto exclude closures with nearby branches as documented in Benson et al. (2024). We find qualitatively and quantitatively similar results.
In columns (3) and (4), we report results for regressions that include all failed-bank branches as a robustness check. The results are similar. In particular, the point estimates for the PE effects are smaller in magnitude, which is consistent with the fact that, on average, PE investors acquire lower quality failed banks. Failing to account for this would underestimate the value-added of PE investors.
We next examine bank performance using deposit growth in local branches. Deposits are an important measure of bank performance, as they reflect a bank's ability to lend and ultimately to generate profits (Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022)). Conceptually, this analysis is straightforward to design—we simply need to compare deposit growth of branches acquired by PE investors with deposit growth of branches acquired by other banks. The challenge, however, relates to the closing and consolidation of acquired branches. For example, assume there are two failed-bank branches, A and B, in Durham County, NC, and that they are acquired by PE and another bank, respectively. Assume that the acquiring bank already has a branch, C, in Durham. Branches B and C could have complex interactions post-acquisition such that it would be inaccurate to simply track deposit levels at B or C. For example, branch C could completely absorb B, which would lead us to underestimate the level of deposits that B attracts.
Our first approach is to perform the analysis at the local county level and consider B and C jointly when evaluating deposit growth. Specifically, our unit of analysis is at the bank-county level. The dependent variable is the change in a failed bank's deposits in the county following the acquisition. For failed banks acquired by PE through shelf charters, this change in deposits corresponds to all branches in the local region; for failed banks acquired by other banks or by PE through inflatable charters or with multiple acquisitions, the change in deposits is calculated using both the failed-bank branches and the branches originally owned by the acquirer to account for measurement issues that may arise from reorganization.21
TableVIIpresents the results. In columns (1) and (2), which use the quasi-random sample, we show that within one year following acquisition, there is little difference in the deposit-level changes. This result is intuitive given that changing failed-bank operations takes time. This result also provides additional support for our quasi-random experiment since there are no observable differences between PE-acquired and bank-acquired branches ex ante. As time progresses, the effect becomes stronger. In column (2), the comparisons are between two failed banks’ operations in the same county over the same period. PE-acquired banks realize 35.6-percentage-point higher three-year deposit growth. The economic magnitude is quite striking if we compare the three-year changes to the unconditional mean. Depending on the sample that we focus on, the averages of the deposit growth variables are negative as a national trend or only mildly positive.
