In columns (3) and (4), we report results estimated using the full non-quasi-random sample of failed banks. The results are qualitatively similar with somewhat lower magnitudes. As discussed in the branch closure analysis above, this is likely due to the fact that PE's acquisition strategy focuses on weaker banks to begin with, making it harder to detect a performance difference if such selection is not controlled for.
Of course, for most failed banks, the operations of the failed banks are combined with the acquirer's pre-existing bank operations—continuing our example, B and C are jointly considered. Accordingly, an alternative approach that we follow is to focus on a smaller sample of bank-county combinations in which the acquirer bank does not already own a branch in the acquired failed bank's region. In other words, we study cases in which there is not a branch C. We report the results in TableVII, columns (5) and (6). After isolating the performance of failed-bank branches themselves, PE-acquired banks continue to have higher deposit growth.
Given the above results, a natural question that arises is: what explains the increase in deposits and overall bank performance? Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022) provide a framework that decomposes the sources of deposit gains into those from pricing, from branch services, and from productivity. The stability of the branch system, documented in TableVI, and higher deposit interest rates, documented in Panel B of TableVIIusing RateWatch data following Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), suggest these forces drive the higher deposit performance. In theInternet Appendix, we leverage Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam's (2022) structural framework to formally quantify the deposit gains from various channels. We find that 45% of the change can be attributed to the pricing effect from interest rates, 10% to the branch network and services, and 45% to the increase in productivity.
The banking sector plays an important role in the local economy. PE's ability to maintain branch operations and attract deposits therefore suggests that the positive changes in bank branch operations also lead to better local economic recovery. We test this hypothesis at the county level using a model adapted from equation (2) that is aggregated to the county level.
In particular, we compare two counties with a failed-bank branch in the same year (both of which were bid on by PE investors and banks), where one bank branch was quasi-randomly allocated to an incumbent bank acquirer and one to a PE acquirer. The quasi-random design in this setting helps alleviate concerns about better performance arising mechanically from PE investors making purchases in worse-hit areas, in which case a return to more “normal” conditions would look more dramatic for them. To cleanly identify the results, we also focus on those counties that have only one pure treatment, that is, we omit counties that have both bank acquisitions and PE acquisitions.
Our first set of measures relates to small business lending. Small business lending is an important function of local banks that has important spillover effects for local economic growth (Berger and Udell (1995)). Our analysis exploits both the extensive margin of small business lending and its intensive margin. TableVIIIpresents the results. In Panel A we use our preferred quasi-random sample. In column (1), we show that within the three years after a PE acquires a local bank, the number of small business lending deals in the county experiences 32% higher growth compared to the number in other counties. In column (2), we show that the increase in the aggregate amount of small business lending is also higher in counties in which the failed bank was acquired by PE.
The increase in the amount of small business lending could be due in part to the pricing of the loans. In column (3), we show that on average SBA loans are priced lower when PE investors intervene with the banks. The economic magnitude is roughly 32 basis points, which is 5% lower than the nonconditional mean of 6.4% reported in TableV.22In column (4), we show that the average deal size increases only mildly, suggesting that the effect operates more through the extension of credit to a larger number of small businesses than through the issuance of larger loans to a small set of small businesses. In Panel B, we report results using the full sample. InInternet AppendixFigureIA.1, we show the parallel trends of these outcome variables before and after acquisitions. In TableIA.Xin theInternet Appendix, we study SBA lending at the bank-county level and find consistent results. In TableIA.XIin theInternet Appendix, we additionally show that the increase in small business lending is not limited to SBA loans, which we use in our analyses due to comprehensive coverage and detailed variables. We find similar patterns using general Community Reinvestment Act (CRA) lending data.
In TableIX, we consider a set of regional variables that focus more on the recovery of local economic conditions. This analysis uses the same regression framework as TableVIII. We report results using both the quasi-random sample and the full sample. In columns (1) and (2), the outcome variables are job creation rates. We find that both the total number of jobs created and job creation in the startup sector are significantly higher in regions in which failed banks are acquired by PE investors. In columns (3) and (4), we examine the level of personal income. We find that the change in personal income is higher, in terms of both total personal income and the level of per capita income.
The magnitudes of the economic impacts—increased lending and faster regional economic recovery—appear to be large. There are several potential explanations for this result. First, we find that PE-acquired failed banks shift their overall lending portfolios away from C&D lending and more aggressively toward business lending in the form of C&I loans, which helps explain the significant change in SBA lending. (SeeInternet AppendixTableIA.XII.) Second, we find evidence consistent with a productivity spillover effect brought by PE acquisitions of failed banks in the local banking market—banks in counties with a PE-acquired failed-bank branch have increased efficiency. (SeeInternet AppendixTableIA.XIII.) This could also be a channel behind the difference in local economic recovery.
Another way to examine the value of PE acquisition is by studying the cost of failed-bank resolution incurred by the FDIC. Here we are interested in whether PE-acquired banks claim more losses to the FDIC. The shared loss agreements with the FDIC typically grant failed-bank acquirers a period of time to claim a part of the losses from covered loan portfolios of the failed banks. Commercial loan portfolios have five years of loss share coverage, with an additional three years of reporting to share any recoveries. Single-family loan portfolios have 10 years of loss share coverage.
If PE-acquired banks perform more poorly in managing the loans in their covered portfolios, we would expect these banks to claim more losses to the FDIC. To test this, we use proprietary data from the FDIC on the total losses claimed by each acquirer with a shared loss agreement on the failed-bank portfolio. We aggregate the claim amounts made and paid out during the coverage period of the agreement. We then scale this aggregated amount by the total assets covered by the agreement to obtain a measure of total claimed loss rates comparable across banks.
TableXpresents the results. After controlling for the fact that PE investors acquired less healthy banks using the selection variables, the estimated differences in claimed losses are virtually zero. In other words, PE investors do not appear to cost the FDIC more than other failed-bank acquirers. We include results for both the total reported losses on the portfolio and the amount of the losses incurred by the acquirer net of loss-sharing payments made to the acquirer by the FDIC. Note that when using our quasi-random sample (columns (3) and (6)), the number of observations drops to 38 banks that meet our criteria and have loss coverage on their loan portfolios. As a result, the analysis has low power, but it is reassuring that the economic magnitudes confirm prior results using the full sample.
PE, as financial investors, are not in the business of permanently running a bank, although they were able to stabilize the failed banks they acquired during the crisis period and regulators had no requirements that PE acquirers exit after a certain period of time. How did PE investors exit these investments, and how did these PE-acquired banks reintegrate into the banking sector? We hand-collect information on the exit of PE-acquired banks from FDIC structure reports on bank merger activity. We obtain details on merger deal values, IPO activity, and any additional rounds of acquirer funding from S&P Global Market Intelligence. Note that this analysis is more informative at the acquiring bank level (consortium level) because different failed banks are consolidated into one bank after being acquired by the same consortium under a given charter.
TableXIsummarizes the exit outcomes of PE-acquired failed banks. Out of 19 banks/consortia, 15 have been acquired or consolidated by other commercial banks, two went public through IPO, one is still under PE operations, and one was liquidated.23Looking at the bank-level counts, which reflect the number of acquired failed banks by the acquiring consortia, out of the 17 banks that were acquired or went public through IPO, PE investors held failed banks for 6.5 years on average. Interestingly, of the 15 acquisitions, 60% were acquired by a local bank, that is, a bank that overlaps with the footprint of the acquired bank at the zip code level. The number is even higher if we consider banks that overlap at the county or state level.
These findings are consistent with the overall interpretation of PE investors’ role in the resolution of failed banks. PE investors acquire risky, failed banks that incumbent banks, which themselves may be in distress, may not have the appetite or capital to acquire. PE investors therefore fill the funding gap and then turn around the failed banks by hiring the necessary expertise. Once the turnaround has been completed, these banks can potentially return to traditional bank ownership. Indeed, slightly more than half of the PE-acquired failed banks were acquired by local banks, which were the natural buyers but were not necessarily in a position to make these acquisitions themselves at the time of failure (Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017)). This finding is also consistent with PE studies showing that PE investors are potentially temporarily the optimal buyer but eventually exit after adding their own value (Kaplan (1991)).
We calculate the return by comparing consortium funds raised with exit prices. Ideally, one would use the total cash disbursement, but this is unobservable in some cases in our data. When these data are not available, we instead rely on total committed capital to proxy for the cash disbursement component in the internal rate of return (IRR) calculation. Given capital disbursement is lower than committed capital, our IRR calculation provides a lower bound on returns. We also supplement this with data on reported additional private placements or offerings from S&P Global Market Intelligence, particularly when additional funds were raised to contribute to additional failed-bank purchases. Announced deal values for mergers are obtained from S&P Global Market Intelligence as well. We find that the deal multiple is 2.11 on average and 2 at the median. We also calculate a rough IRR of 12% after accounting for the holding period, with adjustment for the interim additional funding. This is a good PE return: based on recent estimates from Brown et al. (2015), PE funds of similar vintages earned an IRR below 8%. During the same sample period, annual returns to U.S. public bank stocks were 5.7% (over the 2008 to 2014 period, SIC code = 6020). Admittedly, this return and the PE IRR are not directly comparable due to the lack of proper risk adjustment and the fact that PE-acquired failed banks are very different from publicly traded BHCs. These comparisons nevertheless suggest desirable returns to investors.
Taken together, the evidence thus far paints a positive picture of the role of PE investors in stabilizing the financial system by participating in the process of failed-bank resolution. We next ask how PE intervention can bring about these positive changes to the acquired failed banks, given that PE investors are not bankers. We attempt to answer this question by exploring the management human capital of the PE-acquired failed banks. To do so, we manually collect information on the CEOs of the PE-acquired failed banks from publicly available sources on the Internet, such as company bios, professional profiles, and feature articles.
TableXIIpresents our findings. PE investors hire ex-bankers to manage the acquired failed banks. These CEOs are externally hired, consistent with general patterns in PE-funded companies (Gompers, Kaplan, and Mukharlyamov (2023)). Those bankers have on average nearly 30 years of experience in the banking industry, and more than half of them were CEOs of other banks before being appointed to the failed banks. More than 60% of the CEOs had experience in the local area of the failed bank, and nearly 70% had experience working in a community bank. Interestingly, more than a third of the hired CEOs specialized in turnaround management and troubled and distressed assets.
How economically important is this PE participation? We perform a simple back-of-the-envelope calculation based on proprietary FDIC cost estimates used to evaluate auction bids from all failed-bank deals. The FDIC estimates its cost for each submitted bid in determining the least-cost resolution strategy. Starting with the total gross assets of the failed bank and taking details of each submitted bid into account, it subtracts the expected losses on assets and expected expenses from receivership, as well as any claims on the receivership. The bidder's proposed premium on deposits or discount on assets is included as well. We use these proprietary estimates in our calculation to build on the counterfactual world in which no PE acquisitions occurred.
For all PE-acquired cases, we suppose one of the following two cases apply: (i) if there is at least one non-PE bidder, the best non-PE bidder wins, where the “best” means the lowest FDIC-estimated cost of resolution, and (ii) if there is no other non-PE bidder, the bank is liquidated by the FDIC, which as receiver must pay off insured depositors and dispose of the assets. The additional cost incurred to the FDIC without PE-acquirer participation thus can be roughly estimated by calculating the gap between PE acquirers and the counterfactual outcomes. For case (i), we observe 29 failed banks for which we can calculate the difference in estimated FDIC resolution costs for the winning bid compared to the runner-up. In addition, we observe eight cases in which the runner-up bid in a PE acquisition was also from a PE bidder. In these cases we measure the cost difference between the winning bid and the next best non-PE bid. The aggregate savings are estimated to be around $2.34 billion. For case (ii), we observe 25 failed banks that had no other non-PE bids with a cost below the liquidation cost and would have had to be liquidated by the FDIC. The total savings to the FDIC of those PE acquisitions over liquidation costs is estimated to be $1.29 billion.
In sum, the total cost savings to the FDIC from PE participation is estimated to be around $3.63 billion ($2.34 billion + $1.29 billion). The FDIC estimates that the total cost to the DIF of bank failures during the crisis was about $73 billion.24PE participation therefore helped reduce the FDIC's cost of failed-bank resolution during the crisis by nearly 5%.
One caveat here is that in this simple back-of-the-envelope calculation, we implicitly assume that bank bidding behavior remains the same in a world without PE. Given that the failed-bank bidding process is a sealed-bid auction that does not even disclose the potential bidders, removing PE investors from the bidder set should have at best a mild impact. In addition, this assumption likely leads us to underestimate the benefit of PE participation—without PE competition, banks would likely bid even lower prices, expanding the wedge with PE bid prices.
Although our main focus is on the value of PE acquisitions, our analysis can also help us examine three potential costs, which, if large, could offset the positive effect of PE. First, PE investors may lend excessively to their other portfolio companies at low cost. We do not find this to be the case given the regulatory constraints outlined in SectionI. Second, PEs may not be capable of running financial institutions, which would lead to low operational performance. This is exactly what we try to address in TablesVItoIX, in which we find no evidence to support such costs. Third, PE may draw excessively on the shared loss agreement, leading to high costs for the FDIC. In TableX, using proprietary loss-sharing data from the FDIC, we show that this is not the case.
To summarize, the evidence reported in this section is consistent with the view that PE acquisition of failed banks is associated with positive performance effects. It is worth noting that the identified effects could also be due in part to the regulatory scrutiny of PE acquisitions. However, not all PE effects can be attributed to stronger regulatory scrutiny. For example, while PE acquirers were subject to certain differences in supervisory requirements (such as more required capital) for a period after failed-bank acquisition, these requirements were related largely to safety and soundness standards designed to guard against the risk of failure of the newly chartered nonbank acquirers. Branch closures, in contrast, would not present a safety and soundness concern, but rather a concern related to maintaining banking services in failed-bank assessment areas and hence were not subject to different standards across acquirer types. The analysis on management changes also points to a channel through which treatment occurs.
article.5 Conclusion:
This paper studies the role of PE in stabilizing the financial system after the 2008 financial crisis through failed-bank resolution. Using detailed proprietary failed-bank data from the FDIC together with information on PE investors, we provide the first empirical investigation of PE investors’ role in failed-bank resolution—an economically important but potentially controversial role.
An extensive literature explores the effects of PE on firm-level outcomes, such as employment, productivity, product quality, and innovation. However, PE investors’ role in banking, one of the most central parts of the financial system, has been largely unexplored. The role of PE in times of crisis is also worthy of study—unlike normal times, crisis periods see a clustering of bank failures, more stringent regulatory scrutiny, and distressed investors, leading to a distinct market environment and investor behaviors. Because crises are recurring, and lessons from the last crisis will be used to inform the response to the next crisis, academics and regulators seek to understand economic activities and policies during times of distress. Our paper adds to these efforts by examining the interactions between PE and banking in a period of crisis.
We find that PE investors acquired failed banks that were generally underperforming and riskier than bank-acquired banks. PE investors also acquired failed banks when the neighboring banks were also in distress and therefore had lower ability to make failed-bank purchases. Our findings therefore suggest that PE investors fill the capital gap in scenarios in which the natural local bank buyers are themselves distressed or capital constrained. Using a quasi-random empirical design, we find that PE-acquired failed banks recovered as well as those acquired by banks despite being underperforming ex ante, and we find some evidence that they outperform other failed banks along various dimensions. Our quasi-random empirical design further shows positive real effects for the local economy of PE failed-bank acquisitions. Overall, our results suggest a positive role for PE in helping to stabilize the financial system during the crisis through their participation in failed-bank resolution. These lessons are important for the response to future crises in the United States as well as abroad.

article.Institutional Details: The Failed-Bank R:
Bank resolution activities are initiated by a failing bank letter to the FDIC from federal and state banking regulators who monitor the financial condition of banks. Such initiation is triggered when a banking institution becomes critically undercapitalized or insolvent.25
The FDIC then contacts the management of the failing depository institution. The FDIC also engages a third-party financial advisor to conduct a review of the bank's assets and compile initial information. Importantly, in this review process, the financial advisor estimates a loss factor for each asset category using an identified sample of assets. This information is further used by the FDIC to set the reservation value on the subsequent resolution of the depository institution.
During the financial crisis, the FDIC used the P&A resolution method in roughly 95% of cases. These are the transactions studied in this paper. In a P&A transaction, the FDIC uses a process that resembles a first-price sealed bid auction to sell some or all of the assets and liabilities of the depository institution. Only in cases in which the auction does not generate any bids or when the highest bid is below the FDIC's reservation value will the FDIC use an alternative resolution method. The resolution process can be categorized into four steps: marketing and identifying the bidder pool, providing information, bidding, and resolution.
The first step of selling failed banks is to identify a pool of potential buyers that have expressed interest in bidding on failed institutions and that satisfy a list of requirements. To be approved to bid in a P&A transaction, the potential bidder must be a chartered financial institution or an investor group that has received a conditional charter or is in the process of obtaining a “de novo” charter. PE investors fall into the investor group category of bidders. Moreover, the bidding financial institution must be well capitalized and possess a CAMELS26rating of 1 or 2, a satisfactory CRA rating, and a satisfactory anti-money-laundering record.
In the second step, eligible bidders receive notification of an acquisition opportunity, and those who are interested in pursuing the opportunity must sign a confidentiality agreement. The FDIC then provides eligible bidders with an information package on the failed institution. This contains loan information, schedules representing the book value of the items on the failed bank's balance sheet, and operational information. As part of their on-site due diligence, potential bidders can request to review the individual loan documents.
The third step (formal bidding) generally starts 12 to 15 days before the scheduled closing of the failed bank. Bidders can place one or more sealed bids for the failed bank. The FDIC then chooses the bid that is least costly to the DIF27after evaluating all submitted bids using its proprietary least-cost test model. The FDIC selects the bid whose terms entail the least estimated cost for the DIF if those costs are below the reservation value set by the FDIC, which is unknown to bidders. Bids are not submitted as a single “price,” but rather they frequently vary along multiple dimensions for each bid. For example, these pricing dimensions can include the amount of discount applied to the assets, the premium paid for deposits, the amount and types of assets acquired, the type of transaction for purchase (whole bank, loss share, etc.), the extent of any coverage for shared losses, and so on. The least-cost test must take all of these factors into account when estimating the resolution cost associated with each bid for comparison.
In the final step (the failing depository institution enters receivership) the FDIC takes custody of the failed bank's premises, records, loans, and other assets. The priority for paying allowed claims is given to depositors, including the FDIC as subrogee, over other general and unsecured creditors. The DIF took a loss on most bank failures that occurred during the recent financial crisis, suggesting that receivership proceeds do not cover the FDIC's subrogated claim and that the FDIC is the residual claimant in most receiverships (Hynes and Walt (2010)). Between 2008 and 2013, the DIF lost approximately $73 billion due to depository institution failures.28The fund reached a negative balance on an accounting basis during the third quarter of 2009. It has since recovered and stood at $100 billion at the end of 2018, resulting in a reserve ratio of 1.36%.29The FDIC responded to the large losses on its DIF by collecting a special assessment of 5 basis points on all depository institutions and requiring pre-paid assessments to boost the fund's liquidity. In addition, the FDIC updated its risk-based pricing for deposit insurance.30
